criteria.text: The article describes or proposes at least one concrete prompt-engineering operation: a way to construct, structure, sequence, transform, verify, or constrain prompts or model outputs. Merely using an LLM, evaluating an LLM, or naming a prompting family does not by itself satisfy this criterion.

The title or abstract exposes an identifiable prompt structure, instruction, sequence, procedure, or transferable method that a reader could reproduce or operationalize. Reporting only comparative performance of named prompting methods, without describing a transferable technique or structure, does not satisfy this criterion.
The technique targets large language models or agents whose behavior is based on large language models.

The technique is applicable by a practitioner through prompts, instructions, context, inference-time orchestration, or output constraints, without requiring the practitioner to train or fine-tune model weights or learned prompt parameters.
The article discusses LLMs or agents but does not propose or describe an actionable prompt technique.
The article is solely about model architecture, training, fine-tuning, model weights, or learned prompt parameters, without a practitioner-usable prompting component.
The article is a benchmark or model evaluation without a transferable prompt method, prompt structure, or prompting procedure.
The article reports a domain application of an LLM without describing the prompt method in actionable detail.

The article is an opinion, editorial, or survey that does not expose at least one isolable and actionable prompt technique. A survey can avoid this exclusion when its title or abstract actually identifies transferable techniques or structures.
